qwq-32b.text: The patient is a 45-year-old woman with a 3-month history of painless neck swelling, along with weight loss and fever.
The FNAC (Fine Needle Aspiration Cytology) shows a polymorphous population of cells, including lymphocytes, eosinophils, plasma cells, and large binucleate cells with owl-eye nuclei.
First, the neck swelling suggests a lymph node issue.
The presence of multiple cell types (polymorphous) points away from a single lineage malignancy like a lymphoma that's usually more homogeneous.
The key here is the description of the large binucleate cells with owl-eye nuclei.
Owl-eye nuclei are characteristic of Reed-Sternberg cells, which are seen in Hodgkin's lymphoma.
Reed-Sternberg cells are large, have a prominent nucleolus, and the nucleus can have that owl-eye appearance (big nucleoli with a clear halo around them).
The other cells present (lymphocytes, eosinophils, plasma cells) are part of the reactive inflammatory background seen in Hodgkin's lymphoma.
The systemic symptoms like weight loss and fever are B-symptoms, which are common in aggressive lymphomas, including Hodgkin's.
Other possibilities to consider: Maybe a non-Hodgkin lymphoma, but they typically don't have Reed-Sternberg cells.
The owl-eye feature is pretty specific.
Also, Kikuchi-Fujimoto disease can have atypical histiocytes but not Reed-Sternberg cells.
Infectious mononucleosis might have mixed cells but the cytology wouldn't show those characteristic large cells.
So, putting it all together, Hodgkin's lymphoma is the most likely diagnosis here.
